frameworks.name: Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: General-purpose posterior computation that the paper argues humans do not solely rely on.

frameworks.name: amortized inference / recognition model
frameworks.one_line: Learned query-to-posterior map whose parameters adapt to the query distribution.

frameworks.name: resource-rational analysis
frameworks.one_line: Limited computational resources allocated preferentially to high-probability queries.

core_question: Why do human probabilistic inferences deviate from Bayesian optimality in opposite directions across tasks?
key_assumptions: Brain computes posteriors approximately, not exactly
A recognition model maps queries to probability distributions
Recognition model parameters are optimized to minimize average distance to the true posterior
manipulations: Query distribution / type of inference required
Whether deviation manifests as base-rate neglect or conservatism
key_findings: Humans sometimes under-react to prior probabilities (base-rate neglect)
Deviations from Bayesian optimality go in opposite directions across studies
interpretations: Opposing deviations are explained by a learned recognition model that allocates accuracy to high-probability queries
Apparent inconsistencies reflect adaptation of inference to the query distribution rather than a single general-purpose mechanism
